findings.title: Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) via HTML injection in email content
findings.description: The send-contact-email Netlify function uses user-provided input (name, email, message) directly in HTML without proper sanitization. The message is only processed with a simple newline-to-br conversion. An attacker could inject malicious HTML/JavaScript that would be rendered when the email is viewed in an HTML-capable email client. While the impact is limited to the email recipient (the site owner), this could be used for phishing or social engineering attacks.

findings.title: Email header injection potential via replyTo field
findings.description: The contact form allows user-provided email addresses to be used directly in the replyTo header. While nodemailer typically handles this safely, email addresses should be validated to prevent potential header injection attacks where a malicious user could attempt to inject additional headers.

findings.title: Missing rate limiting on contact form endpoint
findings.description: The contact form endpoint (/.netlify/functions/send-contact-email) lacks rate limiting. An attacker could abuse this to send a large volume of emails, potentially causing SMTP quota exhaustion, increased costs, or being flagged as spam by the email provider.
findings.remediation:
Implement rate limiting at the Netlify function level or use Netlify's built-in rate limiting features. Consider adding:
1. IP-based rate limiting (e.g., max 5 requests per minute per IP)
2. CAPTCHA verification for the contact form
3. A honeypot field to detect bots

For Netlify, you can use edge functions with rate limiting or implement a simple in-memory counter with cleanup.

findings.title: Missing security headers configuration
findings.description: The application does not configure security headers such as Content-Security-Policy (CSP), X-Frame-Options, X-Content-Type-Options, or Strict-Transport-Security (HSTS). While Netlify may add some of these automatically, explicit configuration ensures consistent security posture.
findings.remediation:
Add security headers to netlify.toml:

[[headers]]
  for = "/*"
  [headers.values]
    X-Frame-Options = "DENY"
    X-Content-Type-Options = "nosniff"
    X-XSS-Protection = "1; mode=block"
    Referrer-Policy = "strict-origin-when-cross-origin"
    Content-Security-Policy = "default-src 'self'; script-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline'; style-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline' https://fonts.googleapis.com; font-src 'self' https://fonts.gstatic.com; img-src 'self' data:; connect-src 'self';"
    Strict-Transport-Security = "max-age=31536000; includeSubDomains"

findings.title: Personal contact information exposed in source code
findings.description: The site configuration includes real personal contact information (email: abdulrahmanabdulqawi76@gmail.com, phone: +31687269021) directly in the source code. While this is intentional for a portfolio site, the information is publicly accessible and could be harvested by scrapers or bots.
findings.remediation:
This is an informational finding. If privacy is a concern:
1. Consider using a contact form exclusively instead of displaying direct contact info
2. Use email address obfuscation techniques
3. Consider using a dedicated business email/phone number

For a portfolio site, this exposure is typically intentional and acceptable.

findings.title: External font loading without Subresource Integrity (SRI)
findings.description: The application loads Google Fonts via an external CDN without Subresource Integrity (SRI) hashes. While Google Fonts is generally trustworthy, the lack of SRI means the application cannot verify the integrity of loaded resources.
findings.remediation:
For improved security, consider self-hosting the fonts or adding SRI hashes. However, note that Google Fonts' dynamic CSS generation makes SRI impractical for their service. Alternative approaches:
1. Self-host fonts using a tool like google-webfonts-helper
2. Accept the risk given Google's trustworthiness
3. Add a CSP font-src directive limiting to trusted origins

findings.title: SMTP credentials configured via environment variables (secure pattern)
findings.description: The .env.example file documents the expected environment variables for SMTP configuration. The actual .env file is properly gitignored. This is the correct pattern for handling secrets, but included for completeness to confirm proper implementation.
findings.remediation: No action required. The current implementation correctly uses environment variables and has .env files in .gitignore. Ensure production credentials are configured via Netlify's environment variables UI and never committed to source control.

findings.title: Client-side email validation can be bypassed
findings.description: The contact form relies on HTML5 type='email' validation and basic server-side checks (trim and existence). The server only verifies that email is not empty/whitespace. An attacker can bypass client-side validation and submit malformed email addresses.

findings.title: Error details logged to console in serverless function
findings.description: The send-contact-email function logs the full error object to console when email sending fails. While this aids debugging, in production it could potentially log sensitive information if the error contains credential details or internal system information.

findings.title: No CORS configuration for Netlify function
findings.description: The Netlify function does not explicitly set CORS headers. Netlify functions default to same-origin requests, but explicit CORS configuration provides clearer security boundaries and allows the function to be called from the deployed site.

topRisks: XSS vulnerability in email HTML content via unsanitized user input
Missing rate limiting on contact form could enable email abuse
summary.recommendation: This portfolio website demonstrates good security practices overall. The codebase is a client-side React application with a single serverless backend function for contact form handling. The most actionable finding is the potential XSS in the email HTML content (SEC-001), which should be addressed by properly escaping user input before including it in HTML email bodies. Additionally, implementing rate limiting on the contact form endpoint would prevent abuse, and adding explicit security headers would harden the application's security posture. No critical vulnerabilities were identified - the application follows modern frontend security patterns, properly handles environment variables for secrets, and uses standard React patterns that inherently protect against many common vulnerabilities.
